figures.name: Amma
figures.gloss: The single supreme god who shaped the world from an egg of his own thought, setting the first seed of creation spinning within it; having made all things he withdraws above the firmament he raised.
figures.facet: A creator who makes by inward vibration rather than by hand or word — the cosmos as Amma's egg trembling into form, a maker known through his geometry more than through any cult.
figures.provenance: The cosmogony is well attested in Dogon ritual and Griaule–Dieterlen ethnography; the figure of Amma is secure, distinct from the contested astronomical glosses layered onto it.

figures.name: The Egg of Amma
figures.gloss: The cosmic egg (aduno kine, 'the world-egg') in which Amma planted the first seed; from its spiralling, vibrating germination all the categories of creation — the 266 signs, the eight grains, the directions — unfold.
figures.facet: Creation as a seed swelling toward a perfect helical figure: the Dogon render the whole made world as a single image-bearing egg, so that cosmology and geometry are the same act of reading.
figures.provenance: Central to Dogon cosmological symbolism; the egg-and-spiral imagery is well documented, while specific star-correlations remain academically contested.

figures.name: Nommo
figures.gloss: The first beings Amma made: amphibious, twinned, water-dwelling ancestors of perfect androgynous order. One Nommo is sacrificed and dismembered to purify the disordered cosmos, then resurrected and multiplied to bring water, speech and life to the earth.
figures.facet: A redeemer who is also the twins and the sacrifice in one body: the slain-and-risen saviour is plural and watery, his dismembered parts scattered to seed the world's directions before he rises whole.
figures.provenance: The Nommo sacrifice-and-resurrection is a core, well-attested Dogon mythologem; the 'amphibious instructors from Sirius' reading is the contested Griaule-derived layer and is not relied on here.

figures.name: Ogo / the Pale Fox
figures.gloss: The impatient firstborn who broke from his egg before completion, rebelling against Amma's order; his premature, incestuous descent introduced disorder, dryness and death, and he is changed into Yurugu, the Pale Fox who wanders the bush.
figures.facet: The fall told as prematurity, not pride: disorder enters the world because one twin could not wait to be finished — and the very fox who ruined creation becomes the Dogon's truest diviner, his pawprints in the sand read as oracle.
figures.provenance: Ogo/Yurugu and the fox-divination (yurugu/sand-table) are well attested; the precise astronomical exegesis attached to his egg is the contested material.

figures.name: The Eight Ancestors
figures.gloss: The eight primordial Nommo ancestors, four male and four female pairs, from whom the lineages of humankind descend; they carry the eight seed-grains and the original speech down to the earth in the celestial granary.
figures.facet: Humanity descended not from one first man but from a counted set of eight paired ancestors — the human family rendered as an ordered, gendered octave rather than a single Adam.
figures.provenance: The eight ancestors and eight grains are firmly attested structural features of Dogon cosmology.

figures.name: The Celestial Granary
figures.gloss: The world-system descended from heaven by the Nommo: a stepped pyramidal granary whose ordered compartments hold the eight grains and map every creature and direction — the cosmos as an architecture lowered to earth on a thread.
figures.facet: The axis of the world imagined as a storehouse: order is a granary's grid, and to build a house, a field or a village is to re-inscribe the whole cosmic plan in miniature.
figures.provenance: The granary-descent and its geometric exegesis are central to Dogon ritual architecture, documented in Griaule and in standing village structure.

figures.name: Lébé
figures.gloss: The first ancestor to die, swallowed and then revived by a Nommo in serpent form; his death sanctifies the earth and binds the living to the soil, and the Hogon priest tends the altar where his life is renewed.
figures.facet: Death enters as gift, not curse: Lébé's dying makes the ground holy and arable, so that mortality itself is the seed by which the people are rooted to the land they farm.
figures.provenance: The Lébé cult and the Hogon priesthood are well-attested living institutions of Dogon religion.

figures.name: The Sigui
figures.gloss: The great masked festival held once every sixty years, commemorating the first death and the descent of speech; its procession passes from village to village across years, renewing the covenant between the living, the ancestors and the made world.
figures.facet: A calendar measured in human lifetimes: the Sigui's sixty-year beat means most who dance it do so only once, the rite itself binding mortality to a cosmic cycle no single life can see whole.
figures.provenance: The sixty-year Sigui cycle is a well-documented Dogon institution; the claim that its period tracks the orbit of Sirius B is the contested astronomical interpretation, flagged as speculative.

edges.note: Within the tradition, Amma and the egg he thinks into being are one act of creation — the maker and the world-seed he sets spinning, inseparable.
Amma's first and perfect creatures are the Nommo; the supreme maker recedes and the watery twins become the working redeemers of his creation.
Ogo the impatient twin and the perfected, sacrificed Nommo are the two outcomes of Amma's paired creation — disorder broken loose against the order restored by sacrifice.
The eight ancestors are themselves Nommo offspring who carry the grains and speech to earth — the twin redeemer multiplied into the human lineages.
The eight ancestors descend in the celestial granary bearing the eight grains; ancestors and architecture come down to earth as a single ordered system.
Lébé, the first to die, is swallowed and revived by a Nommo in serpent form — the dying-rising redeemer extended into the founding ancestor of the soil.
The Sigui commemorates the first death and the gift of speech that Lébé and the ancestors brought — the rite that re-enacts the founding mortality.
The dismembered-and-resurrected redeemer whose scattered body fertilises the world — a dying-rising saviour rebuilt to renew creation, across unrelated families.
A primordial being sacrificed and parcelled out so that the ordered cosmos and its categories arise from his body — primordial-sacrifice as the engine of creation.
Both render the first divine reality as androgynous and paired before differentiation; a reach across families, flagged — twinned primordial wholeness.
Divine twins who pass through death to set the world right — but the Dogon twins are watery, androgynous redeemers where the Maya twins are hunters who outwit death.
A perfect being voluntarily sacrificed and resurrected to purify and redeem a disordered creation; a structural rhyme, flagged, with no contact implied.
Neighbouring West-African supreme makers who create and then withdraw from the world's affairs — the deus otiosus shared across the region, no proven common descent.
A supreme sky-source that recedes into impersonal remoteness above the working powers — the withdrawn high-god across unrelated families.
The single ground from which all categories of being unfold by inward vibration rather than external act; a reach toward Brahman as the source, flagged.
Both place an undifferentiated primal whole at the origin from which the ordered world emerges; world-egg beside the unseparated sky-earth, offered cautiously.
The infinite undifferentiated source contracting and unfolding into the categories of creation — world-egg as a rhyme with Ein Sof's emanation, flagged.
The disorder-bringing trickster who is nonetheless indispensable to divination and the working of fate — but Ogo's mischief is a fall, where Esu's is a necessary gate.
The kin-trickster whose impatience and rebellion introduce death and disorder into a made world — the adversary-from-within across families.
A primordial figure who seizes from the creator before the appointed time and so alters the human lot; a reach, flagged — premature theft against ordered patience.
Both pair a disordering trickster-power against an ordering creator-sibling; offered cautiously, the adversary as the necessary counterweight to order.
The first ancestor whose death sanctifies the earth and binds the people to the soil — mortality made the root of agriculture and lineage.
Lébé's altar is tended by the Hogon, the priest-king of Amma's order — the first death folded into the standing cult of the supreme maker.
Both seed humankind from a counted, ordered origin laid out in directions and grains/rivers — but the Dogon count eight paired ancestors against a single first couple; a flagged structural rhyme.
The world ordered along a single descending axis lowered from heaven — granary-architecture beside world-tree as the frame that holds the cosmos.
A stepped, tiered cosmic structure that maps every direction and being onto an axis; a reach across families, flagged.
Both lay the cosmos out as a quartered, direction-bearing plan radiating from a central order; offered cautiously as a cosmogram-rhyme.
A recurring ritual that re-enacts the founding passage through death to renew the covenant of the world; a distant structural parallel, flagged.
Both encode the whole cosmos as a single geometric figure to be read — the world-egg's spiral beside the quartered board; a flagged cosmogram-rhyme, no contact.
